coredata.dc:description: Phosphorene (BP), a two-dimensional (2D) material composed of monolayer or few-layers of black phosphorous (bBP), has recently emerged as a promising candidate for biomedical applications due to its unique electronic, optical, and mechanical properties. This review presents an overview of the theoretical and experimental investigations on the use of BP in various biomedical applications including drug delivery, biosensing, bioimaging, tissue engineering, and its incorporation into various treatment modalities. BP is an excellent candidate for drug delivery applications due to its tunable bandgap, large surface area, and puckered structure. In addition, due to the intrinsic photoacoustic and fluorescence properties of BP, it is regarded highly for biosensing and bioimaging applications. Researchers have taken advantage of its bioimaging capabilities to develop novel synergistic and theranostic treatment modalities, achieving excellent results. Furthermore, the mechanical and chemical properties of BP make it suitable for tissue engineering applications, particularly in the regeneration of bone tissue. The review also discusses concerns regarding the application of BP in biomedicine, including potential toxicity.

head.ce:title: Phosphorene: A novel nanomaterial revolutionizing biomedicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphorene (BP), a two-dimensional (2D) material composed of monolayer or few-layers of black phosphorous (bBP), has recently emerged as a promising candidate for biomedical applications due to its unique electronic, optical, and mechanical properties. This review presents an overview of the theoretical and experimental investigations on the use of BP in various biomedical applications including drug delivery, biosensing, bioimaging, tissue engineering, and its incorporation into various treatment modalities. BP is an excellent candidate for drug delivery applications due to its tunable bandgap, large surface area, and puckered structure. In addition, due to the intrinsic photoacoustic and fluorescence properties of BP, it is regarded highly for biosensing and bioimaging applications. Researchers have taken advantage of its bioimaging capabilities to develop novel synergistic and theranostic treatment modalities, achieving excellent results. Furthermore, the mechanical and chemical properties of BP make it suitable for tissue engineering applications, particularly in the regeneration of bone tissue. The review also discusses concerns regarding the application of BP in biomedicine, including potential toxicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Following the synthesis of graphene in 2004, the field of nanomaterials has emerged into the highlight [1]. The remarkable properties of nanomaterials have started a new era in material science, impacting nearly every scientific and engineering discipline [2]. Particularly, the application of nanomaterials in biomedicine can bring about momentous advancements [3]. Many nanomaterials have high biocompatibility, are environment friendly, have properties distinct from their bulk counterparts, and allow precision engineering of those properties as well [4,5]. The field of nanomaterial-based drugs and biomedical applications is rapidly evolving, with some drugs already available today and many more undergoing clinical trials with promising results [6,7].

An emerging material in this dynamic field is phosphorene (BP), a two-dimensional buckled nanomaterial synthesized from bulk black phosphorus (bBP). bBP is a layered material composed of phosphorous atoms with sp
3 hybridization, held together by weak van der Waals (vdWs) forces. Through exfoliation, bBP can be reduced to monolayer or few-layer BP. The structure of BP, as shown in Fig. 1 , is notably anisotropic: it exhibits a puckered configuration along the armchair direction, while adopting a bilayer arrangement in the zigzag direction. Additionally, the in-plane and out-of-plane arrangements in BP exhibit distinct bond angles and bond lengths, contributing to its unique structural characteristics. The orthogonal unit cell has lattice parameters of a = 4.52
Å
and b = 3.31
Å
, which vary depending on the number of layers [8,9]. The dihedral (bBP = 102.42°, BP = 103.51°) and hinge angles (bBP = 96.16°, BP = 96.00°) are similar to bBP but also show layer-dependent variations [10]. Consequently, the geometric properties of BP can be tuned by controlling the number of layers, offering potential for tailoring its characteristics for specific biomedical applications. However, some properties like the P-P covalent bond length remain consistent regardless of the number of layers [11].

First synthesized in 2014, BP is one of the few nanomaterials that has an established synthesis process for efficient large-scale production of monolayer to few-layer BP [12,13]. It can be synthesized using both top-down and bottom-up techniques [14]. BP has several allotropes named
α
-,
β
-,
γ
-,
δ
-,
ψ
-,
θ
-, and
η
-BP which have been subsequently discovered and studied [15–17]. BP's excellent electronic, optical, and mechanical characteristics render it a highly appealing contender across multiple fields, including biomedicine, electronics, and energy storage [18–20]. It has a high surface-to-volume ratio, making it an excellent platform for sensing biomolecules and drug delivery applications [21]. With its stacked layered structure and weak vdWs interlayer interactions, it has been acknowledged as the most stable 2D layered material [22]. Monolayer BP has Young's modulus of 44 GPa and 166 GPa in the armchair and zigzag orientations, respectively, indicating its remarkable flexibility compared to graphene and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) [11]. Moreover, its high carrier mobility (
∼ 10
3
c
m 2
/ V − s
) and tunable bandgap make it an attractive material for bioimaging and photothermal therapy [23,24]. Furthermore, the unique surface chemistry of BP allows for the functionalization of its surface with various atoms or molecules and enhanced adsorption-based interactions, enabling specific targeting and enhanced therapeutic efficacy [25,26]. Table 1 provides a brief comparison of the properties of BP with other 2D nanomaterials. It is evident that the properties of BP fill the gap between graphene and TMDCs, which has been a highly desirable attribute [27]. However, one of the drawbacks of BP is that it undergoes degradation in ambient conditions as the layers of BP are held together by weak Keesom forces [28]. Therefore, BP-based systems may require protection to prevent degradation which can be achieved through coating in certain applications [29].

Although there are several review papers discussing specific biomedical applications of BP, a comprehensive review of the applications, covering both the first principles and experimental investigations is currently lacking [25,55,56]. This paper aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the theoretical and experimental research into the biomedical applications of BP. We also discuss the challenges and opportunities associated with the use of BP-based nanomaterials in biomedicine, including their biocompatibility, toxicity, and long-term safety. Theoretical studies on BP have mainly focused on using first-principles methods such as density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to predict the properties of BP-based nanomaterials and their interactions with biological systems. They provide an efficient method of eliminating non-viable interactions and systems while achieving results with acceptable accuracy for comparison with experimental results [57]. Experimental studies have focused on the synthesis of BP and BP-based nanomaterials, and their characterization using a range of techniques, including biological environments via various in vivo and in vitro analysis techniques. A considerable proportion of the theoretical and experimental investigations examined revolve around the detection and treatment of cancer, as they constitute the majority of biomedical research conducted on BP. However, we have also analyzed investigations into antibacterial activity, theranostics, tissue engineering, and diagnostics using BP, which are also important biomedical applications.

BP is a prominent 2D semiconducting nanomaterial that sets itself apart with its unique mechanical, geometrical, electrical, and thermal properties [58]. Additionally, these properties can be finely modified by applying mechanical stress, electrical or magnetic field, defect engineering, doping, and changing temperature, while some properties vary with the number of layers and exhibit directionality [58]. Such attributes make BP an ideal candidate for the demanding field of biomedicine.

First-principles calculations offer a reliable, efficient, and inexpensive technique to analyze the efficacy of nanomaterials for drug-related applications. Herein, we briefly note the current progress in investigations into BP-based drug delivery applications employing first-principles calculations.

In an attempt to uncover the potential biomedical applications and cytotoxicity of BP, Wei et al. utilized MD simulations to examine the interactions of BP nanosheets with palmitoyloleoylphosphatidylethanolamine (POPE) membrane [59]. BP nanosheets reportedly penetrated the POPE cell membranes and removed the cell's internal lipids safeguarding the nanosheets from oxidative degradation. Given that BP nanosheets tend to oxidize in an aerobic medium, a more recent MD study from this group inspected three oxidation modes of concentration 2, 5, and 10 % of BP oxide (PO) nanosheets with the POPE membrane [60]. Similar destructive extraction and diffusion of phospholipids on the POPE membrane, dismissing its integrity was revealed. In contrast, another group reported that α-phase BP carbide (α-PC) is unable to thoroughly extract lipids from a 1-palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (POPC) membrane [61]. This has been attributed to the preference of fatty acid ends of lipids to bind only along the groove regions of α-PC. The damage to the membrane caused by α-PC was localized, and the membrane recovery rate was faster than the penetration, leading to an intact POPC membrane.

The target-specific drug delivery capability of nanomaterials is instrumental in cancer treatment. Many anticancer drugs cause severe toxicity by destroying healthy cells, significantly reducing the efficacy of the drug [62]. In 2019, Amina et al. used BP to deliver chlorambucil [63], a drug recognized for its adverse side effects such as seizures [64] and bone marrow suppression [65]. Using B3LYP/6-31G∗∗ level of theory, their results indicated weak p---H bonds in BP-chlorambucil interaction with a dipole moment of 2.49 D. The weak bonds facilitated drug release, and it was concluded that the delivery system holds significant potential. In order to investigate the effects of doping on the potency of BP as a drug carrier, Tahereh et al. examined pristine and metal (Na-, Ca-, and Fe-) doped BP as a carrier for mercaptopurine and fluorouracil anticancer drugs [66]. According to the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) level theory with 6-31G (d) and 6-311G (d, p) basis set calculations, mercaptopurine interaction is relatively weaker (adsorption energy Eads = −0.15 eV at a distance of d = 2.968 Å) than fluorouracil of – (Eads = 0.18 eV and d = 2.802 Å). The HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (Eg) and electric potentials (μ) of the doped structures were also overall larger compared to the pristine structure. Therefore, doping holds great promise in developing novel drug carriers with BP.

Hassan et al. made several first-principles investigations into the efficacy of BP nanosheets as a carrier for anticancer drugs [67–69]. In 2015, the group performed a DFT investigation into a blue BP monolayer (β-P)-based drug delivery system for the anticancer drug 6-tioguanine (TG) in a water solvent environment [67]. The largest Eads observed was −122.08 kJ/mol, indicating physisorption. Subsequent MD simulations with a larger β-P monolayer (173 atoms) revealed that β-P penetrated and diffused successfully into the POPC bilayer cell membrane, and the TG molecule desorbed from β-P, incorporating itself into the cell membrane [67]. Next, this group tested a polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized black phosphorous nanosheet to deliver the anticancer drug Doxorubicin (DOX) [68]. The drug interacted with the PEI-BPH surface via strong pi-pi interaction (DOX-BPH and DOX-PEI interaction energies were −180 and −50 kJ/mol, respectively), which was strengthened by DOX-PEI hydrogen bonds. To mimic tumor cells (high pH environment) [70], the group then analyzed the drug release in an elevated pH medium, which revealed that the increased solubility of the polymer at high pH leads to drug release. The group also elucidated the competency of BP as a dual drug carrier [69]. They demonstrated that cancer drug molecules DOX and Paclitaxel (PTX) form stable complexes with BP nanosheets facilitated mainly via vdWs interactions. In their comparison, DOX (PH-DOX pair interaction energy, Eintp = −144.85 kJ/mol) and PTX molecules (PH-PTX; Eintp = −845.98 kJ/mol) interacted more strongly with BP than BN nanosheets.

The principal goal of anticancer drugs is to delay or halt the growth of cancer by interfering with the DNA synthesis of cancer cells. To achieve this, many anticancer drugs mimic the structures of nucleobases, as illustrated in Fig. 2 [71]. Moreover, nucleobases play a vital role in detecting diseases and microorganisms, diagnostics, and developing emergent technology of biological interfaces [72–74]. This motivated Diego et al. to study the interaction of BP with five DNA/RNA nucleobases (adenine (A), cytosine (C), guanine (G), thymine (T), and uracil (U)) and three hydrogen-bonded base pairs (HBP) (CG, AT, and AU) [72]. Dispersion corrected DFT theory (dc-DFT) predicted the physisorption of all the nucleic acid bases. In terms of the Eads of the nucleobases, the order was G > C ≥ A ≥ T > U, where the last three orders may differ based on the theory employed [75–77]. The HBP order, on the other hand, was CG > AT > AU. Upon comparison with graphene, the structures were 27 % more stable, implying the superiority of BP. Based on the changes in the PDOS spectra, polarizability, and charge transfer characteristics after the interaction, they concluded that BP could act as a rapid sensor for the nucleobases. Defect engineering and doping are recognized methods for significantly altering the properties of BP [78]. To utilize this, a second study from this group doped BP using Si, Ti, Cu, I, and their interactions with nucleic acid bases were explored [79]. Except for Si-BP, all the nucleobases interacted with the dopant atoms with short bond lengths ranging from 1.9 to 2.2 Å and Eads in the range of 1.2–2.9 eV. The Ti-BP and Cu-BP structures showed promise as nucleobase sensors owing to their nucleobase-sensitive bandgap. Further research by Yelda et al. investigated the interaction of pristine, gold cluster, and Ti decorated BP with a total of six amino acids and nucleic acid bases [80]. Pristine BP interacted with the molecules via weak vdWs interaction (binding energy (Eb) range 0.65–0.84 eV) and nominal charge transfer, leading to a minimum distance of ∼3 Å. After decoration, the AUx cluster induced a metallic transition in BP while Ti induced a direct to indirect transition, and both were able to induce a permanent magnetic moment in BP. Additionally, the efficacy of BP and its allotropes as toxic gas sensors has been reiterated in several theoretical studies [81–83].

A study combining DFT, and cluster models examined a BP complex functionalized with the antitubercular chemotherapeutic drug pyrazinamide (PZA) [84]. The PZA drug molecule was physisorbed on the BP nanosheet with Eb = −0.66 eV, dipole moment of 5.13 D, distance of 3.42 Å, and charge transfer of 0.17 e (Mulliken). The cluster model revealed an Eg of 1.52 eV, which remained approximately unchanged after PZA adsorption. These results were later confirmed by Wanling et al., GGA-PBE level of theory [85]. In addition, they investigated the interaction of BP with another tuberculosis drug called isoniazid (INH). A linear variation of Eg with pH values for the INH/BP complex was demonstrated which could be utilized to sense monolayer BP in photothermal therapeutics.

Traditional antibiotic drugs often annihilate beneficial bacteria in the body [86]. Their accumulation in the animal body or the environment can also cause serious issues. Moreover, the number of antibiotic-resistant bacteria has increased steadily in recent years [86,87]. The development of new and improved treatment options against bacteria is thus urgently needed. To investigate the antimicrobial efficacy of BP, Meijuan et al. incorporated silver nanoparticles (AgN) into BP nanosheets [88]. According to their DFT calculations, the added AgNs facilitated the adsorption and activation of O2, enhancing the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation capability of the nanohybrids. On top of photocatalytic disinfection, AgNs increased affinity toward bacteria, resulting in synergistic antibacterial action. Subsequent in vivo analysis produced results consistent with their DFT findings. Inspired by preceding research utilizing carbon nanomaterials as sensors [89,90], Chandiramouli et al. opted for (10,10) armchair blue phosphorene nanotube (β-PNT) to identify and sense two antibiotics found commonly in food items, oxytetracycline (OC) and sulfapyridine (SP) from water medium [91]. The studied complexes had Eads ≈ 1.241 eV, implying chemisorption, which is suitable for sensing the molecules from water bodies. Recovery time is a key figure in measuring the quality of sensing materials [92]. However, at room temperature, the calculated recovery time by the group was too long to be feasible and only dropped to milliseconds at 600 K. In 2019, the group relaxed a black phosphorene nanoring (α-PN) by rolling up a BP nanosheet (lattice parameters: a = 4.57 Å, b = 3.29 Å) along the armchair direction using dispersion corrected GGA/Beck86/Lee-Yang-Parr level of theory [93]. Next, the interaction of the stabilized structure with four kidney disease biomarker volatiles (KDBV) was examined. Positive charge transfer engendered a change in the resistance of α-PN, making it suitable for use as a chemi-resistor for KBD sensing. Moreover, up to ∼67 % change in Eg and up to 0.42 Å−3 electron difference density after interaction was reported. Such significant variation in electronic properties further strengthens the efficacy of α-PN as a sensor to detect kidney diseases.

The puckered structure and large surface area of BP provide several suitable sites for interaction with drug molecules. Due to the unique optical properties of BP, interactions with ultraviolet (UV) and near-infrared (NIR) light can evoke electromagnetic radiation mediated drug release. Moreover, the electronic properties of BP can be finely modified via methods such as doping and geometry customization [94]. Motivated by these excellent properties and the promising results reported from first-principles investigations, numerous experimental studies on BP have transpired in recent years.

BP has demonstrated significant potential in various drug delivery systems. This section discusses several innovative platforms utilizing BP, which are summarized in Table 2 . One notable application is in the treatment of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a life-threatening condition. The risk of DVT grows exponentially with age [95]. Heparin, the natural anticoagulant that has been used against DVT, has a high risk of hemorrhage and suffers from a short active time in the body [96]. To tackle this, an iron oxide nanoparticle (Fe3O4) and PEI decorated BP delivery platform (PEI/Fe3O4@BP) was constructed by Huan et al. [96]. At 500 μg/mL concentration of the unfractionated heparin sodium (UH), the delivery platform indicated a peak loading efficiency of 450 % and in vivo analysis revealed ≈87 % release of UH following 7h NIR irradiation. Both in vitro and in vivo analysis found that PEI/Fe3O4-BP was successfully enriched via a magnetic field, affirming the magnetic targeted accurate delivery hypothesis. A second study from the same group was inspired by the cork-opening mechanism of champagne bottles [97]. Their hypothesis predicted the formation of bubbles in blocked vessels to aid in the thrombolytic action. A dual chain reaction of drug release (thrombolytic) and formation of bubbles was confirmed via in vitro analysis. Subsequent in vivo assessment using a murine acute jugular vein thrombosis model confirmed the absence of any visible clots in jugular veins after a 12h treatment. Furthermore, BP-based nanoparticles were investigated for their potential use against bacterial infections by creating a BPQD delivery platform [98]. The BPQDs were decorated with polyethylene glycosylated (PEG) chitosan (CS) nanospheres to carry the antibiotic amikacin (AM). At a concentration of 30 μg/mL, the PEG@CS/BPQDs-AM illustrated a 90 % antibacterial efficiency against four bacteria strains that were tested in vitro. Moreover, the biocompatibility of the molecules was established in both in vitro and in vivo assessments.

The fight against diseases such as Alzheimer's, Huntington's, Parkinson's, and type II diabetes mainly involves inhibiting the aggregation of amyloid proteins with β-sheet structure or ensuring their disassembly [105]. Siqi et al. used thioflavin-T (ThT) fluorescence, far-UV circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and MD simulations to test the efficacy of BPQDs against the amyloid fibrillation of insulin [99]. Even a low concentration (50 ng/mL) of BPQDs had a significant effect, while a dosage of 100 ng/mL totally inhibited insulin fibrillation. According to their AFM analysis, a 500 ng/mL concentration of BPQDs, when co-incubated with insulin, could annihilate even mature fibrils after seven days. Similar inhibiting capability was shown by a nano delivery system (PEG-LK7@BP) against Aβ42 fibrillogenesis employing a peptide inhibitor LVFFARK (LK7) [100]. 100 μg/mL concentration of PEG-LK7@BP reduced the ThT fluorescence intensity to 17.0 %, later confirmed by AFM images. MTT analysis results, similar to that of BPQDs, concluded that PEG-LK7@BP was not only nontoxic but could also potentially subdue Aβ42 cytotoxicity to a certain degree.

Nanomaterial-based drug delivery systems are particularly crucial in the treatment of cancer. Traditional treatment modalities such as radiotherapy, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy lack specificity and are known to have adverse effects, which reduces their efficacy as a treatment method [106]. For example, platinum-based drugs such as Oxaliplatin (OP) and cisplatin (CP) have been frontline choices in the fight against multiple cancer types for more than 20 years despite their adverse side effects [107,108]. Michaela et al. loaded both OP and CP into BP nanosheets to investigate their efficacy against ovarian cancer [109]. In the A2780 human ovarian cancer cell line, a somewhat synergistic effect was observed in the case of OP-BP with 21 % of A2780 annihilation at a concentration of 30 μg/mL. Yuanyuan et al. [101] conducted another study focusing on CP for treating triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), a type of cancer with no targeted therapies available yet [110,111]. They investigated CBPH (CP@BP/polydopamine (PDA)- hyaluronic acid (HA)) in vitro at low pH (5.0), with the presence of H2O2 and 808 nm laser irradiation to simulate an irradiated tumor biological environment. A cumulative release of 78.9 ± 2.8 % CP was achieved which led to elimination of the main tumor and inhibition of metastasis. However, in vivo response to low pH and high H2O2 was deemed insufficient. The anticancer activity of BP nanosheets was reported by Wenhua et al. to exhibit superior selectivity to that of conventional chemotherapeutic drug DOX [102]. Their in vivo assessment affirmed the tumor suppression capability of BP enabled by selective degradation in cancer and normal cells. However, the instability of BP in ambient conditions remains a critical hindrance in its biomedical applications [112]. Xiaowei et al. overcame this limitation by modifying BP with PDA, resulting in the co-delivery agent BP-R-D@PDA-PEG-Apt as shown in Fig. 3 , which was used to simultaneously deliver P-gp siRNA (small interfering RNA) and DOX [103]. The PDA coating successfully protected BP from oxidative degradation. Both in vitro and in vivo investigations showcased excellent tumor growth inhibition based on multimodal therapy. In another study, Wei-Qiang et al. doubly functionalized BPs covalently with mitochondrial targeting peptide (MTP) and acid-labile PEG, leading to an increase in BP stability and tumor accumulation ability [113]. In vivo results affirmed that after the dissociations of MTP and PEG in the cell surface, BPs successfully targeted mitochondria, causing cell death under NIR irradiation.

To improve the performance of radiotherapy, Leung et al. used the nanoagent poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) to deliver radiosensitizer BPQDs (PLGA-SS-D@BPQDs) [114]. The targeting molecule Arg-Gly-Asp-Cys (RGD) enabled the nanoagent to distinguish tumor cells from normal ones and achieve site-specific release. Following the release of BPQDs at lower pH values (6.8), the BPQDs successfully generated ROS and inhibited cancer growth (reduction in relative tumor volume up to 2168 % - in vivo) under X-ray irradiation. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that using a BPQDs platform camouflaged with platelet membranes can enhance the efficacy of the poorly performing anticancer drug Hederagenin. This approach also provided supplementary anticancer effects that induce mitochondria-mediated apoptosis and autophagy in tumor cells [115]. Another group loaded BP and the anticancer drug docetaxel into PLGA to create the multifunctional nanoagent BP/DTX@PLGA [104]. Their in vivo and in vitro analysis affirmed good biocompatibility, and the nanoagent demonstrated excellent synergistic inhibition of cancer metastasis.

Phototherapy is a modern therapeutic technique that relies on irradiating active components to generate heat (photothermal therapy (PTT)), ROS species (photodynamic therapy (PDT)), or to achieve targeted therapy by combining photodynamic therapy with immunotherapy (photoimmunotherapy (PIT)) for the treatment of various conditions [116,117]. Nanomaterial-driven phototherapy has the potential to realize significantly improved curability and efficacy of drugs at a much lower dosage and reduced side effects [118–120]. BP has garnered significant interest in phototherapy owing to its exceptional carrier mobility, biocompatibility, targetability, and efficient generation of ROS and heat upon irradiation [55]. Additionally, its tunable bandgap enables a broad absorption spectrum that ranges from UV to visible light to NIR [55]. Table 3 summarizes the key studies and results discussed in this section, which are organized into three subsections: photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, and photoimmunotherapy.

In photodynamic therapy (PDT), photosensitizers accumulate selectively in the cancer cells. When irradiated with light of proper wavelength, the photosensitizers produce ROS through photochemical reactions with oxygen molecules leading to cell death. This provides an efficient, non-invasive, and relatively safe way to treat cancer.

Hui et al. were the first to demonstrate the potential of BP as a PDT agent [121]. In their study, singlet oxygen production had a quantum yield of about 0.91, higher than most of the previously reported photosensitizer agents. Moreover, being a metal-free agent; BP was deemed promising for PDT. Achieving target-specific delivery of photosensitizers and the hypoxic condition in tumors are two major concerns in PDT. Tumor-specific accumulation has been established for BP through prior investigations [122–124]. A prominent example of this is the inflammation-based target signal and positive feedback loop showcased by Yujie et al. [125]. In terms of the hypoxic condition, various solutions have already been suggested. Jiang et al. decorated BP with artificial catalase Pt nanoparticles to reduce the expression of the hypoxia-inducing factor-1α [122]. They achieved complete regression of 4T1 tumors (in Mice) following PDT. In another approach, Jintong et al. designed a BP-based dual-triggered oxygen self-supplying PDT agent by incorporating Fe-protoporphyrin IX (heme) [124]. The heme generated in situ O2 from H2O2 in tumor cells resulting in an 8.7-fold (hypoxic cells) and 7.5-fold (tumor cells) increase in PDT efficacy. In a follow-up study, the group designed another self-supply agent R-MnO2-FBP (Rhodamine B-encapsulated manganese dioxide-fluorescein isothiocyanate labeled peptide-functionalized black phosphorus) [123]. By monitoring the oxygen self-supply process using Mn2+ and RhB dye, properly timed application of laser irradiation led to 51.6 % apoptosis in hypoxic cells. Furthermore, singlet oxygen generation was achieved by Da Zhang et al. employing BP-PEL/AuNPs (BP hybridized with Au nanoparticles through polyetherimide (PEL) bridge), which also enhanced PTT performance through localized surface plasmon resonance [126]. In contrast, Shanyou et al. prepared Apt-BMSF@Pt (TLS11a DNA aptamer-decorated and Pt nanoparticle incorporated BPQD-hybridized mesoporous silica framework) to specifically target hepatocellular carcinoma and achieved specificity and self-supply of oxygen in hypoxic cells [127].

Photothermal therapy is based on the NIR irradiation of photosensitizing agents to generate heat for the thermal ablation of cells. Previously proposed metallic and carbon-based nano photosensitizers have been criticized for their lack of biocompatibility, photothermal efficiency, and mechanical stability [137]. However, considering the high therapeutic efficiency of PTT with minimal invasiveness and controlled dosage, the search for new photosensitizers is ongoing. Given the tunable bandgap [138], high photothermal conversion efficiency [129].

Jundong et al. made several investigations into BP-based nanoparticles for PTT. In 2015, the group synthesized ultrasmall BPQDs of dimensions: lateral
∼
2.6 nm; thickness
∼
1.5 nm [128]. Results indicated an extinction coefficient of 14.8 Lg−1cm−1 at 808 nm and photothermal conversion efficiency of 28.4 %. Furthermore, the nanoparticles demonstrated significant anticancer behavior against C6 and MCF7 cancer cells. In the subsequent year, they utilized BPQDs/PLGA nanospheres to enable enhanced permeability and retention, resulting in a significant improvement in tumor-specific accumulation and a prolonged blood circulation time [129]. As a result, excellent in vivo PTT performance was achieved in BALB/c nude mice with subcutaneously injected MCF7 (human breast cancer) cells and in BALB/c mice with subcutaneously injected B16 (mouse melanoma) cells, where the tumor volumes were allowed to reach 200 mm³ and 100 mm³, respectively. The group then focused on the issue of cancer recurrence [130]. They incorporated hydrogels onto BP to develop a PTT based treatment strategy consisting of spraying a minuscule dose of the agent combined with NIR irradiation (0.5 Wcm−2) to eliminate residual cancer cells. The PTT agent could also kill >99.5 % of bacteria with only about a minute of irradiation, eradicating wound infection post-surgery. The efficacy of BP@hydrogel structures as PTT agents has been further reinforced by complementary investigations from other groups [139–144].

Besides hydrogel modified BP sensitizers, numerous novel PTT agents have been put forward. Xin et al. synthesized erythrocyte membranes (RMs) coated BPQDs (BPQD-RMNV nanovesicle) as a PTT agent [131]. When irradiated, the agent-induced apoptosis in basal-like breast cancer cells in addition to recruiting dendritic cells (DCs) following the release of antigens. Since basal-like breast cancer has a high mutation rate [145], the group additionally administered anti-PD1 antibody, which successfully achieved a significant CD8+ T cell response. Similar promising results have been obtained in combinational PTT-immunotherapy investigations [136,146]. Other combinations of BP-based PTT agents with cancer therapeutic drugs (e.g., PTX) have also demonstrated excellent targeted and combinational anticancer behavior [147,148]. A novel “three-in-one” polymer-grafting strategy was reported by Junyang et al. [149]. Using triphenylphosphine, their nanoplatform successfully targeted tumor mitochondria and initiated cell death through PTT both in vitro and in vivo. On the other hand, Miaomiao et al. used mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) to transport PLGA/BPQD PTT agent to U251 glioma tumor cells [150]. The in vitro and in vivo results indicated significant improvement in targeting and anticancer activity compared to bare PLGA/BPQD. In treating severe conditions such as Parkinson's disease (PD), the blood-brain barrier (BBB) is a prominent obstacle [151]. By loading BP with the ligand lactoferrin (Lf) and Paeoniflorin (Pae) where the Lf provided the ability to target the brain, BBB was successfully traversed after NIR irradiation [132]. Besides treatment, BP-based PTT agents can also be utilized in sensing applications. Fig. 4 illustrates the basic working principles of typical electrochemical and optical biosensors. When Au nanoparticles are incorporated into BP nanosheets (BP-Au), the PTT conversion efficiency has been reported to increase, in some cases, by more than 12.9 % over pristine BP [152,153]. According to Guangcun et al., BP-Au can cause hyperthermia induced cancer eradication [152]. More importantly, due to the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity of BP-Au, the PTT effect can be monitored in real-time, and the molecular fingerprint of tumor cells can be successfully identified [152]. Shijie et al. were the first to develop a quantitative immunochromatographic sensor (PT-ICS) using BP-Au [153]. They were able to detect enrofloxacin (ENR) (veterinary antibiotics) within 0.03–10 μg/L with a detection limit of 0.023 μg/L. This paves the way for future investigations into photothermal sensing applications with BP.

Immunotherapy, although promising, suffers from significant drawbacks. Lack of targetable tumor-specific antigens (TSAs), defects in TSAs presentation, tumor heterogeneity, autoimmune diseases, and allergic reactions are just some of the many obstacles that must be overcome before cancer immunotherapy can be an effective therapeutic solution [154–157]. The discovery of nanomaterials such as BP has introduced novel ways of overcoming the limitations of immunotherapy. In this regard, PIT is a promising new treatment modality as it improves upon results obtained by PTT. BP nanomaterials have been extensively studied for their potential in PIT primarily due to their excellent PTT performance.

The axis blockade agent anti-CD47 (aCD47) demonstrates excellent antitumor capability. However, it is unable to generate enough phagocytic activity and antitumor immune response [158]. By harnessing the PTT and tumor-specific immunological stimulating effects of BP in combination with aCD47, Zhongjian et al. achieved local and systematic tumor inhibition that also showed promise for metastatic inhibition [133]. Another study reported promising in vitro and in vivo performance of BP-PEG and imiquimod (R837) (used as a photothermal conversion agent and immunoadjuvant) after integrated PTT and PIT [134]. Moreover, the generation of tumor-associated antigens was revealed in their in situ analysis, which resulted in a stronger immune response. To replace the traditional inorganic nanomaterials used in combinational cancer therapies, Hanh et al. developed BP nanocomposites incorporating dabrafenib, immune checkpoint inhibitors, and cancer-targeting antibodies (CXCR4) [135]. The cancer targeting and inhibiting capability of the bare nanocomposites were further improved following PTT, facilitated by the presence of BP. The efficacy of PEGylated HA acid-BP combinational therapy incorporating PDT, PTT, and PIT has also been reported [136]. More recently, a study demonstrated that a BPQDs-based hydrogel loaded with the drug zoledronate could controllably release the drug under NIR irradiation and stimulate gamma-delta (γδ) T cells, killing up to 70 % of MDA-MB-231 cells with an E/T ratio (effector to target) of 5:1 [159]. BP nanoparticles, therefore, have immense potential in photoimmunotherapy and warrant extensive study to establish new BP-based PIT treatment techniques.

Pathogenic bacteria are an alarming health concern worldwide [160]. Also, the number of antibiotic-resistant bacteria has been increasing [87]. Considering the development period of conventional antibacterial solutions, the fear of an epidemic looms across the world. Therefore, novel alternatives for tackling bacterial infections are required. Nanomaterial-based antibacterial platforms (nanobiotics) are a promising solution that has recently gained significant attention.

BP can be used either independently or in combination with other drugs or molecules to provide antibacterial action. Shaw et al. demonstrated that pristine BP can be used as an effective antibacterial coating at nanogram concentrations by generating ROS [161]. Moreover, studies from other groups have illustrated that coatings incorporating BP alongside other agents display antibacterial efficacy up to 99.9 % [162,163]. Due to the optical properties of BP, it can further serve as an effective antimicrobial phototherapy (APT) agent. Elisa et al. highlighted that pristine BP, and BP conjugated with metals, hydrogels, and drugs such as vancomycin can be used in APT [164]. As discussed in section 2.2.2.2
Photothermal Therapy, similar antibacterial PTT activity has been demonstrated by Jundong et al. with BP@PLEL hydrogel [130]. In a more thorough antibacterial investigation, İlknur et al. found that when irradiated by NIR, BP/Au nanocomposites completely eradicate Enterococcus faecalis and inhibit its biofilm formation by up to 58 % [165]. Alternatively, when ZnO is added to form ZnO-Au-BP nanocomposites, they not only exhibit excellent antibacterial activity against the multidrug-resistant bacterial species Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) but also prevent the development of resistance to the treatment after multiple consecutive dosages [166]. Significant improvement in the synergistic antimicrobial performance of BP was achieved in vivo by doping BP with Ag nanoparticles [88]. The Ag nanoparticles led to a substantial increase in affinity toward bacteria, induced charge separation and suppressed concurrent carrier recombination in BP. Zhenyu et al. reported superior antibacterial efficacy of exfoliated BP nanosheets against S. aureus (methicillin-resistant) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) compared to graphene and MoS2 [167]. Alternatively, Chang et al. achieved superior efficacy against S. aureus both in vitro (99.97 %) and in vivo by covalently functionalizing BP with fullerene C70 [168]. In another study, the synergistic action of quaternized chitosan (QCS) modified BP (BP-QCS) was evaluated in vitro in these two species and in vivo against S. aureus [169]. The PTT-antibacterial synergistic action led to 95 % inactivation in vitro and 98 % in vivo, after just 10 min of irradiation with a minuscule dose of 75 μg/mL (in vitro).

However, not all BP-based antibacterial treatment methods require irradiation. Using nanoscale transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs, Abhijit et al. demonstrated that BP could disrupt the cellular membrane and integrity of E. coli with up to 95 % efficacy [170]. Similarly, according to Pan et al., in BPs@CDs (CD = incorporated cationic carbon dot), while laser irradiation improves the antibacterial performance of the nanoagent, even without irradiation, they display a substantial antibacterial effect that could prompt accelerated regeneration of skin tissue and wound closure [171]. Zhibin et al. showed that when titanium aminobenzenesulfanato complexes (Ti-SA4) are incorporated into BPs, the resultant Ti-SA4@BPs eliminate nearly all bacterial populations within 3 h at a relatively low dosage of 50 μg/mL without any laser irradiation [172]. Furthermore, Shaw et al. provided evidence of the intrinsic antibacterial efficacy of BP [161]. They reported that few-layer BP demonstrates broad-spectrum efficacy against bacteria and fungi, including resistant strains such as C. neoformans. Therefore, BP-based nanomaterials have demonstrated significant antibacterial action that has the potential to replace traditional treatment methods and to produce next-generation medical devices, surface coatings, sterilization methods and more.

Tissue engineering is a treatment method that has recently attracted much attention, particularly due to its regenerative features. Nanomaterials have already been widely studied in developing new tissue engineering techniques [173–175]. BP has been identified as an effective agent for wound healing applications, operating on various levels such as antibacterial activity, angiogenesis, and re-epithelialization [176,177]. Complexes based on BP can serve as an "artificial skin," shielding wounds from the environment while also promoting wound repair [178].

Xu et al. demonstrated that PDA-modified BP, when incorporated into gelatin methacryloyl hydrogels, significantly enhances the electrical conductivity of the hydrogels, leading to the differentiation of MSCs into neural-like cells [179]. This offers valuable insights into tissue engineering for other electroactive tissues, such as cardiac, neural, and skeletal muscle tissues. Qian et al. also acknowledged the remarkable electrochemical properties of BP and engineered a BP/polycaprolactone scaffold, which maintained electrical conductivity for up to 4 months in vivo [180]. Their scaffold effectively promoted neurogenesis and facilitated the regeneration of 20 mm peripheral nerve defects without causing severe toxicity, providing new insights into the regenerative capabilities of BP. Another group achieved the regeneration of neurons in the spinal cord using hydrogel-encapsulated BPQDs [181].

The utilization of BP in bone-tissue engineering has been one of its critical applications, primarily because of its favorable properties, such as electrical conductivity, stimulation of biomineralization, biocompatibility, and biodegradation. Phosphate ions or phosphonates, which are naturally occurring in bone tissue, are also byproducts of BP biodegradation in the body and facilitate osteointegration and osteogenesis [182]. Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune disease that causes the progressive destruction of cartilage and bone. A thermoresponsive hydrogel was created by Wenzhen et al. by combining BP and platelet-rich plasma-CS to manage the condition [183]. The BP-containing hydrogels successfully delivered ROS to the inflamed joints and supplied the raw materials necessary for osteanagenesis, providing articular cartilage protection. Miao et al., on the other hand, proposed a hydrogel matrix for bone tissue engineering, using BP nanosheets as building blocks [144] This hydrogel matrix achieved reinforcement of crosslinking networks, enhanced mineralization capabilities of the networks, NIR photothermal antibacterial activity, and promoted osteogenesis even in the absence of osteoinductive factors. Another BP-based hydrogel for tissue engineering has been reported with the biodegradable polymer oligo (poly(ethylene glycol) fumarate) as the crosslinking matrix [184]. Fig. 5 illustrates how this hydrogel provides a constant supply of phosphate ions along with improved proliferation, adhesion, and osteogenic differentiation of pre-osteoblast cells (MC3T3), which was further enhanced via electric stimulation. Other studies have also reported promising outcomes using hydrogel-based methods [185–187].

3D printing has been a transformative technique in bone regeneration, enabling the fabrication of scaffolds and stents that are biocompatible and can be tailored to specific bone fracture sites [188]. In the treatment of bone defects resulting from tumor resection procedures, a scaffold-based strategy was reported that involved cryogenically 3D printing multiple functional agents, including BP and DOX, to sequentially suppress tumor recurrence and augment tissue regeneration [189]. During in vivo bone regeneration, the presence of BP substantially decreased the long-term toxicity of DOX. The ability of BP to supply inorganic phosphate ions and facilitate biomineralization has been reiterated by Yingqian et al. [190]. In another approach, SrCL2 and PLGA were incorporated into BP, creating the drug delivery platform BP-SrCl2/PLGA to assess NIR-triggered bone regeneration prospects [191]. After a precisely controlled dosage of Sr2+ via NIR irradiation in rats with femoral defects, in vivo investigation produced the highest levels of bone regeneration compared to other control groups. 3D-printed scaffolds have also been employed in osteosarcoma treatment, with BP coating applied onto glass scaffolds and implanted into mice [192]. NIR irradiation induced tumor ablation, while BP degradation facilitated calcium phosphate (CaP) formation, promoting subsequent bone regeneration.

Current treatment methods for serious diseases such as cancer, Alzheimer's, bone defects, and depression suffer from issues such as poor specificity, low efficiency, excessive cost, toxicity, and lack of biocompatibility [193,194]. Therefore, the focus of researchers has shifted to synergistic therapy, where existing or new treatment methods are combined into a single treatment option. This can lead to much higher efficiency, lower exposure to compromising treatment components and a lower risk of recurrence [193,195]. Moreover, the potency of synergistic therapy can be further improved by employing nanomaterials with superior properties compared to traditional materials [196].

The anticancer efficacy of BP was evaluated by Feifei et al. by creating BP-PAMAM@DOX (Poly amiodoamine) via electrostatic adsorption [197]. A pH and NIR irradiation triggered release was affirmed using High Performance Liquid Chromatography. Both in vitro and in vivo analysis suggested chemo-photothermal synergistic anticancer activity against 4T1 cells. According to Li Gao et al., Zn2+ ions increase the stability of BP, and modification of BP with PEG-Apt also improves blood circulation time and tumor specificity leading to efficient performance against prostate cancer [198]. Similar synergistic dual therapy anticancer performance of pristine BP has been reported by other research groups [147,189,199–201]. In 2016, Wansong et al. studied pristine BP for synergistic photothermal-photodynamic-chemotherapy of cancer [202]. According to their findings, the photostability of BP is superior to that of indocyanine green which a clinically authorized molecule, and that BP has a high loading capacity up to 950 % (for DOX at 1 mgML−1). Their synergistic tri-therapy achieved 95.5 % of tumor growth inhibition. Wenquan et al. introduced a unique plug-and-play nanorization technique comprising of ultrasonic bubble busting of BP flakes (coarse) that can produce uniform BP (
∼ 40
n m
lateral dimension with
∼
0.15 polydispersity index) [203]. The resulting nanosheets were found to be an effective chemo-photo-immunotherapy agent for synergistic action against colorectal cancer [203]. Fluoxetine is a commonly prescribed antidepressant drug [204]. However, its antidepressant effects, similar to other medications for depression, can take up to months to reach effective levels [205]. Liguo et al. utilized BP-Flu nanocomposites and studied their in vivo performance using measurement tests such as the sucrose preference test and suspension test [205]. They reported that BP-Flu could pass the BBB and reduce the treatment time to 2 weeks in the hippocampus.

The majority of biomedical applications require highly precise properties. Consequently, various methods e.g., surface decoration, functionalization, and encapsulation, have been investigated to fine-tune the properties of BP and utilize it in the field of biomedicine. For example, in the PTT treatment of tumors residing deep within the body, the PTT efficiency of BP becomes low due to the limited optical transparency of biological tissues [196]. Decorating the BP surface with gold nanobipyramids has been reported to overcome this issue with a significant increase in ROS generation via plasmon-enhanced light absorption [196]. When BP nanocomposites were formed with Fe3O4 and Au nanoparticles, the resulting nanocomposites reportedly demonstrated the ability to inhibit tumor growth via visualized synergistic therapy induced using magnetic resonance imaging [206]. Deblin et al. anchored copper sulfide nanodot on folic acid (FA)-modified BP to overcome typical limitations of synergistic PTT and PDT (e.g., low solubility in aqueous medium of photosensitizers) [207]. In vivo analysis affirmed the ability of the nanoconjugate to accumulate on cancer cells aided by the folate receptor targeting capability of folic acid and oxidative degradation, allowing renal clearance. Interestingly, the nanoconjugate could also be tracked via in vivo photoacoustic imaging (PAI) facilitated by the generation of high contrast ultrasound signals. Another imaging-guided synergistic dual PTT and PDT treatment method was explored by Xiaoyan et al. [208]. They obtained a 43.6 % PTT conversion efficiency, and their designed complex (BP@PEG/Ce6 where Ce6 = Chlorin e6) could also generate ROS efficiently in vivo. In a follow-up study, utilizing platinum nanoparticles (Pt) as a nanoenzyme, the group created BP/Pt-Ce6@PEG, which was able to efficiently relieve tumor hypoxia and eradicate tumor cells (4T1) in an allograft mouse model [209]. Prior to this study, the synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy performance of Pt@BP had been reported by Jianing et al. against CP-resistant A2780 and HepG2 cell lines [210]. The developed Pt@BP also interacted with DNA in vitro, inducing inter/intra-strand DNA cross-linkage. By growing BiO3 nanoparticles on BP, X-ray induced PDT can be achieved, as affirmed by Hao et al. [211]. The heterostructures were reported to achieve overproduction of ROS, leading to apoptosis of cancer cells and cycle arrest, thus paving the way for synergistic cancer radiotherapy with BP.

The use of polymers and hydrogels has become increasingly popular in creating BP-based treatment platforms, as they offer several advantages. These materials can enhance the overall specificity, stability, efficiency, dosage regulation, biodegradability, and biocompatibility of the platform [212–215]. For example, a hydrogel was formed using dibenzaldehyde-functionalized polymer (DF-PEG) and polyaspartylhydrazide (PAHy) resulting in the injectable DF-PEG-PAHy/BP platform [216]. With exemplary gelation attributes, the delivery system, after being loaded with DOX exhibited smart delivery capability and anticancer activity against MDA-MB-231 cells in vitro and in vivo (BALB/c nude mice). Also, the BP combined PRP-CS hydrogel (thermoresponsive) developed by Wenzhen et al. has been reported to achieve synergistic PTT-PDT dual therapy for RA management [183]. PLGA is a US Food and Drug Administration approved pharmaceutic adjuvant [217]. It was used by Xin et al. to prevent premature oxidation of BP and enable the loading of DOX without hampering its biodegradability [159]. Treatment using BP/PLGA/DOX + NIR irradiation resulted in a cell mortality level of 84.7 % in vitro, and in vivo results indicated complete cancer elimination with no indication of recurrence. Furthermore, the delivery of human telomerase reverse transcriptase (hTERT) has been established as an efficient agent in achieving tumor growth inhibition [218,219]. To improve the low specificity and cellular uptake of traditional approaches, Lei et al. modified BP with PEG and PEI to deliver hTERT siRNA [220]. More than 96 % loading efficiency, robust cellular uptake, and successful delivery of siRNA were affirmed. PEGylated BP has also been shown to increase physiological stability and carry out effective synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy against acute lymphoblastic leukemia [221]. Given the excellent performance of polymer/hydrogel modified BP, such studies continue to attract significant attention in developing new synergistic treatment options [101,195,222].

Shengyong et al. created the first drug delivery system based on BPQDs by employing liposomes, a popular anticancer drug delivery agent, to produce BPQDs@Lipo nanocomposites and deliver DOX [223]. In vitro results indicated more than 90 % cancer cell eradication by the combination of BPQDs@Lipo-DOX and NIR irradiation (10 min). After comparison with control groups, the synergistic effect of BPQDs@Lipo-DOX with NIR was affirmed. In a follow-up study, the group created another BPQD based nanoplatform that exhibited similar excellent synergistic anticancer performance against resistant A549R tumors [224]. In addition, the nanoplatform inhibited the overexpression of the drug-efflux proteins (especially P-glycoprotein) that have been identified as the primary cause of drug resistance [225].

Transfection efficiency (TEF) is a crucial factor in determining the efficacy of gene therapy [226]. Feng et al. engineered a novel nanocarrier that could deliver siRNA into human ovarian teratocarcinoma PA-1 cells [227]. Their treatment method achieved 80 % growth rate inhibition and superior TEF following combined photothermal and gene therapy. Xueyuan et al. encapsulated a BPQD based nanoplatform with red blood cell membranes, promoting improved retention and permeability [228]. The combination of DOX and Kirenol (an anti-inflammatory drug) in the nanoplatform synergistically remodeled the studied tumor microenvironment, inhibited the apoptotic effect of tumor cells, and downregulated inflammatory cytokines interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor
α
that has been associated with tumor progression [229]. A similar BPQD and DOX based antitumor nanoplatform developed by Miaomiao et al. showcased photo and pH-responsive release properties [230]. According to their cellular targeting assay, the tumor-targeting capability of the nanoplatform was significantly improved following surface conjugation of folic acid. Moreover, the assembly of iron oxide carbon dot (Fe3O4) on BPQDs has been reported to induce efficient ROS generation (660 nm laser irradiation), PTT efficiency of 36.2 % (808 nm NIR irradiation), active fluorescence, and significant T2-weighted MRI [231]. Other groups have obtained similar promising results while studying the efficacy of a multifunctional synergistic approach using BPQD based nanoplatforms [104,232–234]. Furthermore, according to Yi Li et al., PEGylayed BPQDs can produce characteristic fluorescence signals in G2 and 4T1 cells, paving the way for bioimaging with BPQDs [234].

The term “theranostics” originates from the combination of therapy and diagnostics. In biomedicine, theranostic nanoplatforms contain both therapeutic and imaging components in a single formulation. The principal objective of theranostics is to provide monitoring of drug delivery, release, and efficacy. This is of immense importance in preclinical/clinical trials, allowing real-time monitoring of pharmacokinetics. Also, it enables personalized medicine and enhances the balance between drug efficacy and toxicity. Notably, many phosphorus compounds, when combined with other materials, have been approved for clinical use and are widely utilized in theranostics [235–237]. BP also possesses several important traits for theranostics, e.g., intrinsic photoacoustic and fluorescence properties.

BP nanomaterials demonstrate exceptional optoelectronic properties, attributed to a bandgap that varies with layer thickness. Thinner BP exhibits sharper peaks in their photoluminescence (PL) spectra [238]. Lee et al. pioneered the development of a new class of fluorescent BP nanodots [239]. At a dosage of 1.0 mg mL−1 for HeLa cells (in vitro), the nanodots showed little to no cytotoxicity and exhibited PL activity in multiple colors (green and blue). Another group showed that it is possible to utilize the large surface area of BP to load fluorescent molecules such as Ce6, enabling bioimaging and synergistic cancer therapy [208]. According to Yuetao et al., BP modified with Nile Blue (NB) dye exhibits strong NIR fluorescence in tumors in vivo [240]. Using fluorometry post-injection, the group was able to model the blood circulation of NB@BP and achieve NIR imaging-guided PTT of cancer. In vivo NIR imaging capable theranostic delivery platform based on BP has also been reported by Wei et al., who used cyanine7 (cy7) as dye [241]. In another study, cy7 N-hydroxysuccinimide ester was used by Lijuan et al. to achieve NIR imaging in BP-DEX/PEI-FA that also exhibited targeted PAI capability (maximum intensity reached 8h post-injection) [242]. In a follow-up study, the group then labeled BP-DEX with technetium-99m (labeling yield: 83.8 %) using the chelating effect of the phosphate groups located on the BP surface [243]. The pharmacokinetics were monitored via SPECT/CT, revealing the PTT efficacy of the administered BP-DEX.

PAI is a powerful modality capable of generating images with high spatial resolution (up to 5 μm) with optical contrast in regions deep within biological tissues (5–6 cm) [244]. Notably, PAI is inherently background-free as only specific tissues absorb the signal, thus overcoming the speckle artifacts commonly encountered in ultrasound imaging [245]. Zhongjun et al. demonstrated that polydopamine-functionalized BPQDs can be utilized for PAI guided PTT against cancer [246]. They utilized the Vevo 2100 LAZR system at a frequency of 40 MHz, 2D gain of 0 dB and PA gain of 28 dB to showcase the potential of BPQDS in PAI. BPQDs coordinated with TiL4 also exhibit excellent PAI capability [247]. At 680 nm, TiL4@BPQDs have a large NIR extinction coefficient, leading to superior PAI performance compared to gold nanorods in the case of MCF-7 tumor bearing mice. Li et al. devised an innovative technique to synthesize BP that is stable in ambient conditions by utilizing noncovalent stacking interaction with 1-pyrenylbutyric acid [248]. The resulting complex showed concentration dependent PAI signal intensity and also enabled anchoring of suitable biomolecules. Sun et al. reported a similar observation of concentration dependent PAI signal intensity using PEGylated black phosphorus [249]. Furthermore, the complex demonstrated tumor-specific accumulation and enabled PAI visualization of the tumor site. In vivo treatment with the complex resulted in complete tumor regression, with no detectable traces of the complex found in the liver or kidneys post-treatment, suggesting excellent biocompatibility and targeted drug delivery.

Kuan et al. achieved quantitative positron emission tomography (PET) guided synergistic chemodynamic (CDT) and PTT by using Cu2+ coordinated BP [250]. PET labeling was realized with 64Cu using a chelator-free method, demonstrating a high labeling efficiency of 99 %. While PET offers excellent sensitivity, imaging techniques like optical coherence tomography (OCT) can be more beneficial in specific theranostic applications due to their cellular-level resolution [251]. Moreover, the photothermal optical coherence tomography (PT-OCT) variant of OCT can produce changes in the optical phase transitions in tissues triggered by changes in the environment, e.g., temperature change due to photon absorption in PT-OCT [251]. A BPQD-based nanoagent for PT-OCT was developed by Haolin et al. that showcased excellent PTT efficacy and performed as a robust contrast agent for PT-OCT bioimaging [252]. The developed agent was deemed biodegradable according to their analysis, which is a rarity for PT-OCT agents and a highly desirable attribute.

One of the popular theranostics techniques investigated thus far consists of incorporating upconversion nanoparticles (UNCPs) with BP based therapeutic platforms [253–255]. NIR irradiation on such platforms can not only serve to activate the nanoplatform but could also generate emissions of higher wavelengths that can be utilized for imaging applications. For example, in a study by Solomon et al., NaYF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 core–shell (UNCP) was used to develop UNCP-BP nanocomposite [256]. This nanocomposite not only eliminated 91 % of cancer cells via synergistic PTT-PDT but also demonstrated very promising magnetic resonance (MR) and upconversion luminescence imaging (UCL) capability. Furthermore, a BP nanocomposite developed using magnetic UNCP, and Ce6 has been reported to provide T1 and T2 MR, fluorescence (facilitated by Ce6), optoacoustic, and ultrasonic imaging [257]. The nanocomposite also activated synergistic PTT-PDT under single 808 nm irradiation. Hence, BP-based theranostic platforms have significant potential in biomedicine.

Before BP can be approved for biomedical applications, several challenges must be addressed, including extensive toxicity and biocompatibility studies to ensure its safety and efficacy, as well as to identify strategies for minimizing associated risks. Due to its superior biodegradability, BP is typically considered to have lower toxicity compared to nanomaterials such as graphene [129,220]. To shed light on this matter, Naziah et al. exposed BP to human lung carcinoma epithelial cells (A549) for 24 h [258]. Using two standard assays, they reported a dose-concentration correlated response with the cytotoxicity levels being generally intermediate to graphene oxides and transition-metal dichalcogenides. While investigating the cytotoxicity of BP in L-929 fibroblast cells, Su-Jin et al. also reported a similar dependence of BP cytotoxicity on the exposure time [259]. Interestingly, at concentrations lower than 4 μg/mL, they found insignificant cytotoxicity levels deemed suitable for biomedical applications. Michaela et al. reported
≈
92.7 % cell viability at concentrations of 12.5 μg/mL [109]. However, at low concentrations of
≈
2.5 μg/mL, instead of cytotoxicity, they observed improved cellular growth. The group suspected that this may be attributed to similar growth capabilities reported in graphene [260]BP may form scaffolds at low concentrations for cellular attachment, leading to increased cellular proliferation.

The blood circulation, biodistribution, and pharmacokinetics of dextran modified BP nanoparticles were examined by Caixia et al. [243]. They ascertained negligible cytotoxicity using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay at concentrations lower than 100 μg/mL, with degradation and excretion via hepatobiliary and kidney clearance pathways. Long-term in vitro biodistribution study from another group also showed complete metabolic degradation of BP [261]. Yinqiang et al. found that surface modification of black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) leads to an increase in their adsorption on the mucin surface which serves as a biological barrier against cell penetration [262]. At the same time, changes in the pH level, ionic strength, and ionic valence, also have notable effects on the adsorption levels. Their work paves the way for understanding the interaction of BPQDs with cellular interfaces and further cytotoxicity assessments.

Contrary to the aforementioned toxicological results, the investigations from other researchers have yielded contrasting results. Using a kidney organoid model, Chengyong et al. [263] affirmed that BPQDs cause nephrotoxicity, while the study of Xuejiao et al. [264] revealed that layered BP could generate ROS and severely affect cell membrane integrity. BPQDs have also been shown to cause acute toxicity, such as a decrease in bone marrow nucleated cell numbers, but subsequent transient self-recovery has also been observed in such cases [265]. Therefore, further research is required to attain conclusive evidence regarding the toxicity and biocompatibility of BP and BP-based materials.

The rapid degradation of BP in biological environments has been a significant hurdle towards its application in biomedicine. However, this can be overcome by methods such as functionalization and coating that enhance its stability [103]. Nevertheless, when considering novel BP or BP-based materials for biomedical applications, degradation remains a crucial factor to consider. In terms of synthesis, scalable synthesis techniques for few-layer BP with large areas are still lacking [266]. Moreover, identifying the exact chemical structure of BP complexes functionalized with other materials also remains a challenge. DFT calculations, microscopy techniques such as 2D NMR, and the development of advanced techniques such as HRTEM will be crucial in this regard.

BP possesses the intrinsic capability to generate ROS upon irradiation. While this is extensively studied for applications such as phototherapy, it raises concerns about potential unintended damage to DNA and proteins in healthy cells. This issue can be avoided by improving the specificity of the agents to minimize off-target effects. Another issue is that studies also often lack data on in vivo immune response to BP. This information can be crucial, particularly in implant and tissue engineering applications. Recent reports of possible immune rejection against BP-based implants necessitate further investigation in this area [267]. Furthermore, the targetability of cancer can be a major challenge due to the dynamic tumor microenvironment. Therapies can inadvertently alter the tumor microenvironment e.g., changes in protein expression within the tumor, creating a negative feedback loop that reduces efficacy [268]. This challenge is further compounded by tumor heterogeneity, where a single tumor can harbor diverse cell populations with varying responses to treatment [269]. Negative feedback can be overcome by employing methods such as PDT and PTT. These therapies can induce acute inflammation, potentially generating positive feedback loops that enhance targeting signals. On the other hand, addressing tumor heterogeneity remains an active area of research. In this regard, multimodal therapies, theranostic platforms, and advanced targeting strategies are promising avenues for future exploration.

BP holds immense promise in revolutionizing biomedicine, potentially surpassing traditional materials like graphene. Its unique and tunable properties pave the way for targeted drug delivery systems with enhanced efficacy. BP's inherent biodegradability and theranostics potential further solidify its promise for biomedicine. Moreover, BP's potential for synergistic therapies, combining multiple therapeutic modalities like drug delivery and phototherapy, offers a pathway to innovative treatment methods. The current theoretical and experimental research on BP shows great promise, particularly in the field of cancer therapies. Multiple studies have reported excellent anti-cancer efficacy even against metastasized and resistant tumors. The antibacterial activity of BP also offers a novel weapon against antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

However, significant challenges remain before widespread clinical use can be realized. Inconclusive evidence regarding BP's long-term toxicity necessitates in-depth biocompatibility assessments. The use of materials such as metals in conjunction with BP also raises concerns about potential bioaccumulation and biocompatibility in long-term use. Scalable synthesis of high-quality BP and controlled mass production of BP-incorporated materials are additional hurdles. Moving forward, future research should broaden its scope beyond cancer, delving deeper into theranostics and tissue engineering applications for the development of regenerative medicine modalities. Researchers should also prioritize determining BP's long-term biocompatibility, biodegradation, alongside assessing targetability, specificity, and immune response when exploring new BP-based therapeutic approaches. Overcoming these challenges will unlock BP's true potential BP and could significantly impact the landscape of biomedicine, offering innovative solutions for a wide range of biomedical challenges.
